title: Read picture graphs
description: Use picture graphs to solve word problems.

assessment_items.question_content:
Mrs. Walls made a picture graph to show how many bug stickers  her students have.  


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many more bug stickers does the person with the most stickers have than the person with the fewest stickers? **  

[[☃ input-number 2]]  more stickers
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD6$ bug stickers.

Student| Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of bug stickers
:- | :-: | :-
Erika| $7$ | $\blueD6  \times 7= \maroonD{42}$  
Javier| $4$ | $\blueD6  \times 4 = {24}$
Putri| $2$ | $\blueD6  \times 2= \greenD{12}$  
Mega | $5$ | $\blueD6 \times 5= {30}$

Erika has the most bug stickers $(\maroonD{42})$.

Putri has the fewest bug stickers $(\greenD {12})$.

$\maroonD{42} - \greenD{12} = 30$
The person with the most bug stickers has $30$ more stickers than the person with the fewest bug stickers.

options.alt: A picture of a ladybug equals 6 bug stickers.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Student and the vertical axis labeled Number of pictures. Each student is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Erika, Javier, Putri, and Mega. The number of bug stickers for each student is represented by the number of pictures of ladybugs plotted on the graph. Erika is represented by 7 pictures of ladybugs, Javier is represented by 4 pictures of ladybugs, Putri is represented by 2 pictures of ladybugs, and Mega is represented by 5 pictures of ladybugs.

assessment_items.question_content:
A lifeguard made a picture graph of the number of shark sightings at different beaches.


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Fill in the blank.**  

High Tides had [[☃ input-number 2]] fewer shark sightings than Wave World in September.
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD3$ shark sightings.

Beach | Number of[[☃ image 1]]| Number of shark sightings
:- | :-: | :-
Sandy Shore | $2$ | $\blueD3  \times 2= {6}$  
$\maroonD{\text{High Tides}}$ | $3$ | $\blueD3 \times 3= \maroonD{9}$
Sunny Escape | $6$ | $\blueD3  \times 6= {18}$  
$\greenD{\text{Wave World}}$ | $4$ | $\blueD3  \times 4 = \greenD{12}$
$\greenD {12} - \maroonD{9} = 3$
High Tides had $3$ fewer shark sightings than Wave World in September.

options.alt: A picture of a shark equals 3 shark sightings.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Beach and the vertical axis labeled Shark sightings in September. Each Beach is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Sandy Shore, High Tides, Sunny Escape, and Wave World. The number of shark sightings in September for each beach is represented by the number of pictures of sharks plotted on the graph. Sandy Shore is represented by 2 pictures of sharks, High Tides is represented by 3 pictures of sharks, Sunny Escape is represented by 6 pictures of sharks, and Wave World is represented by 4 pictures of sharks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Naama graphed how many acorns the squirrels at the nature center ate.  


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**How many more acorns did the squirrel with the most acorns eat than the squirrel with the fewest acorns?**   
  
[[☃ input-number 2]]   acorns
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD3$ acorns eaten.

Squirrel| Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of acorns eaten
:- | :-: | :-:
Nutsy| $5$ | $\blueD3  \times 5= {15}$  
Stripe | $3$ | $\blueD3  \times 3 = {\greenD9}$
Fluffy| $4$ | $\blueD3  \times 4= {12}$  
Scamper | $6$ | $\blueD3 \times 6= \maroonD{18}$

Scamper ate the most acorns $(\maroonD{18})$.

Stripe ate the fewest acorns $(\greenD {9})$.

$\maroonD{18} - \greenD{9} = 9$
The squirrel with the most acorns ate $9$ more than the squirrel with the fewest acorns.

options.alt: A picture of an acorn equals 3 acorns.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Squirrel and the vertical axis labeled Acorns. Each squirrel is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Nutsy, Stripe, Fluffy, and Scamper. The number of acorns for each squirrel is represented by the number of pictures of acorns plotted on the graph. Nutsy is represented by 5 pictures of acorns, Stripe is represented by 3 pictures of acorns, Fluffy is represented by 4 pictures of acorns, and Scamper is represented by 6 pictures of acorns.

assessment_items.question_content:
Yoav graphed the  number of cookies sold at the bake sale. 

![A cookie picture.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffd7c5622c9bc75cdd526ae7465e37c22a21871e) $=6$ cookies

[[☃ image 2]]

**Fill in the blank.**

Peanut butter sold [[☃ input-number 2]] more cookies than Sugar.
hints:
Each ![A cookie picture.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffd7c5622c9bc75cdd526ae7465e37c22a21871e) equals $\blueD6$ cookies.



So, each ![A half cookie picture.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f5fa44481d6c1062e6c1952ab2dfab6778395aad) equals $\greenD3$ cookies.

Let's find out how many pictures are across from peanut butter and sugar.

[[☃ image 1]]

Cookie Type | Number of ![A cookie picture.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ffd7c5622c9bc75cdd526ae7465e37c22a21871e)| Number of cookies
:- | :-: | :-:
Peanut Butter | $4\dfrac12$ | $({4}\times\blueD{6}) + \greenD3=27$ cookies
Sugar | $2$ | ${2}\times\blueD{6}=12$ cookies
$27 - 12 = 15$ cookies
Peanut butter sold $15$ more cookies than sugar.

options.alt: A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of cookies and the vertical axis labeled Cookie Type. Each type of cookie is listed along the vertical axis from the bottom of the axis to the top of the axis as follows: Chocolate Chip, Sugar, Oatmeal, Peanut Butter, and Snickerdoodle. The amount of each type of cookie is represented by the number of cookie pictures plotted on the graph. Chocolate Chip is represented by 6 cookie pictures, Sugar is represented by 2 cookie pictures, Oatmeal is represented by 3 cookie pictures and 1 half cookie picture, Peanut Butter is represented 4 cookie pictures and 1 half cookie picture, and Snickerdoodle is represented by 4 cookie pictures.

assessment_items.question_content:
Arnav graphed the number of bananas sold at different stores.  


[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**Fill in the blank.**

Fruit Zone sold  [[☃ input-number 2]]  fewer bananas than Grocery Town on Tuesday
hints:
Fruit Zone has $\purpleD4$ *pictures* in the graph.    

 Grocery Town has $\greenD6$ *pictures*  in the graph.    

$\greenD6 - \purpleD4 =\maroonD2$  

Fruit Zone has $\maroonD2$ fewer *pictures* in the graph than Grocery Town.
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 1]]

$ \maroonD2$ pictures $\times 5 = 10$
Fruit Zone sold  $10$ fewer  bananas than Grocery Town on Tuesday.

options.alt: A picture of a banana equals 5 bananas.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Bananas sold on Tuesday and the vertical axis labeled Store. Each store is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: Meal Market, Fab Food, Grocery Town, and Fruit Zone. The number of bananas for each store is represented by the number of pictures of bananas plotted on the graph. Meal Market is represented by 2 pictures of bananas, Fab Food is represented by 3 pictures of bananas, Grocery Town is represented by 6 pictures of bananas, and Fruit Zone is represented by 4 pictures of bananas.

assessment_items.question_content:
Neha graphed the amount of cheese her pet mice ate.


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Fill in the blank.**

Whiskers ate [[☃ input-number 2]] fewer grams of cheese than Tiny Toes.
hints:
Tiny Toes has $\purpleD6$ *pictures* in the graph.    
Whiskers has $\greenD4$ *pictures* in the graph. 

$\purpleD6 - \greenD4 =\maroonD2$  

Whiskers has $\maroonD2$ fewer *pictures* in the graph than Tiny Toes.

Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD4$ grams of cheese.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]
 $ = \maroonD2 \times \blueD4 = 8$
Whiskers ate $8$ fewer grams of cheese than Tiny Toes.

options.alt: A picture of cheese equals 4 grams.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Mouse name and the vertical axis labeled Grams of cheese eaten. Each Mouse name is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Whiskers, Tiny Toes, Missy Mouse, and Beady. The grams of cheese eaten by each mouse is represented by the number of pictures of cheese plotted on the graph. Whiskers is represented by 4 pictures of cheese, Tiny Toes is represented by 6 pictures of cheese, Missy Mouse is represented by 3 pictures of cheese, and Beady is represented by 3 pictures of cheese.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.

[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which squirrels ate more than $10$ acorns?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD3$ acorns eaten.

Squirrel | Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of acorns eaten
:- | :-: | :-
Nutsy | $5$ | $\blueD3\times5=15$ acorns
Stripe | $3$ | $\blueD3\times3=9$ acorns
Fluffy | $4$ | $\blueD3\times4=12$ acorns
Scamper | $6$ | $\blueD3\times6=18$ acorns
Nutsy, Fluffy, and Scamper ate more than $10$ acorns.

options.alt: A picture of an acorn equals 3 acorns eaten.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Squirrel and the vertical axis labeled Acorns. Each squirrel is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Nutsy, Stripe, Fluffy, and Scamper. The number of acorns for each squirrel is represented by the number of pictures of acorns plotted on the graph. Nutsy is represented by 5 pictures of acorns, Stripe is represented by 3 pictures of acorns, Fluffy is represented by 4 pictures of acorns, and Scamper is represented by 6 pictures of acorns.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which farm has fewer ponies than Horse Haven?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{3}$ ponies.

Farm | Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of ponies
:- | :-: | :-
Fancy Farm | $5$ | $\blueD{3}\times5=15$ ponies
Pony Pasture | $2$ | $\blueD{3}\times2=6$ ponies
Horse Haven | $3$ | $\blueD{3}\times3=\maroonD9$ ponies
Riding Ranch | $4$ | $\blueD{3}\times4=12$ ponies

Horse Haven has $\maroonD9$ ponies.  Which farms have less than $\maroonD9$ ponies?

$6 < \maroonD9$

So, Pony Pasture has fewer ponies than Horse Haven.

options.alt: A picture of a pony equals 3 ponies.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Farm and the vertical axis labeled Ponies. Farm is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Fancy Farm, Pony Pasture, Horse Haven, and Riding Ranch. The number of ponies in each farm is represented by the number of pictures of ponies plotted on the graph. Fancy Farm is represented by 5 pictures of ponies, Pony Pasture is represented by 2 pictures of ponies, Horse Haven is represented by 3 pictures of ponies, and Riding Ranch is represented by 4 pictures of ponies.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which zoos have more than $5$ elephants?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{2}$ elephants.

Zoo | Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of elephants
:- | :-: | :-
Safari City | $3$ | $\blueD{2}\times3=6$ elephants
Zooville | $4$ | $\blueD{2}\times4=8$ elephants
Animal Land | $5$ | $\blueD{2}\times5=10$ elephants
Tusk Town | $2$ | $\blueD{2}\times2=4$ elephants
Safari City, Zooville, and Animal Land have more than $5$ elephants.

options.alt: A picture of an elephant equals 2 elephants.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Zoo and the vertical axis labeled Elephants. Each Zoo is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Safari City, Zooville, Animal Land, and Tusk Town. The number of elephants for each zoo is represented by the number of pictures of elephants plotted on the graph. Safari City is represented by 3 pictures of elephants, Zooville is represented by 4 pictures of elephants, Animal Land is represented by 5 pictures of elephants, and Tusk Town is represented by 2 pictures of elephants.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.



[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Who drank more than $15$ ounces of milk?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD4$ ounces.

So, each

[[☃ image 2]]

 equals $\greenD2$ ounces.

Person| Number of glasses| Number of ounces
:- | :-: | :-
Daniel | $4\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD4\times4) + \greenD2=18$ ounces
Salma | $3$ | $\blueD4\times3=12$ ounces
Karim | $3\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD4\times3) + \greenD2=14$ ounces
Anna | $5$ | $\blueD4\times5=20$ ounces
Daniel and Anna drank more than $15$ ounces of milk.

options.alt: A picture of a glass of milk equals 4 ounces.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Ounces of milk and the vertical axis labeled Person. Each Person is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: Daniel, Salma, Karim, and Anna. The number of ounces of milk for each person is represented by the number of glasses of milk pictures plotted on the graph. Daniel is represented by 4 and a half pictures of glasses of milk, Salma is represented by 3 pictures of glasses of milk, Karim is represented by 3 and a half pictures of glasses of milk, and Anna is represented by 5 pictures of glasses of milk.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.



[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which locations have the same number of shark sightings?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{3}$ shark sightings.

Location| Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of shark sightings
:- | :-: | :-:
Sandy Shore | $4$ | $\blueD{3}\times4=12$ sightings
High Tides | $3$ | $\blueD{3}\times3=9$ sightings
Sunny Escape| $6$ | $\blueD{3}\times6=18$ sightings
Wave World | $4$ | $\blueD{3}\times4=12$ sightings
Two locations each had $12$ shark sightings.
Sandy Shore and Wave World both had the same number of shark sightings.

options.alt: A picture of a shark equals 3 shark sightings.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Beach and the vertical axis labeled Shark sightings in September. Each Beach is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Sandy Shore, High Tides, Sunny Escape, and Wave World. The number of shark sightings in September for each beach is represented by the number of pictures of sharks plotted on the graph. Sandy Shore is represented by 4 pictures of sharks, High Tides is represented by 3 pictures of sharks, Sunny Escape is represented by 6 pictures of sharks, and Wave World is represented by 4 pictures of sharks.

assessment_items.question_content:
Look at the following graph.

[[☃ image 1]] 
$=10$ apples


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which trees have the same number of apples?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{10}$ apples.

So, each 

[[☃ image 2]]

 equals $\greenD5$ apples.

Tree | Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of apples
:- | :-: | :-:
Tree A | $7\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{10}\times7) + \greenD5=75$ apples
Tree B | $4\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{10}\times4) + \greenD5=45$ apples
Tree C | $6$ | $\blueD{10}\times6=60$ apples
Tree D | $4\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{10}\times4) + \greenD5=45$ apples
Tree E | $3$ | $\blueD{10}\times3=30$ apples
Two trees each have $45$ apples.
Tree B and Tree D have the same number of apples.

options.alt: A picture of an apple.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of apples and the vertical axis labeled Apple type. Each Apple type is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: Tree E, Tree D, Tree C, Tree B, and Tree A. The number of apples for each apple type is represented by the number of pictures of apples plotted on the graph. Tree E is represented by 3 pictures of apples, Tree D is represented by 4 and a half pictures of apples, Tree C is represented by 6 pictures of apples, Tree B is represented by 4 and a half pictures of apples, and Tree A is represented by 7 and a half pictures of apples.

options.alt: A picture of cheese equals 4 grams.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Mouse name and the vertical axis labeled Grams of cheese eaten. Each Mouse name is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Whiskers, Tiny Toes, Missy Mouse, and Beady. The grams of cheese eaten by each mouse is represented by the number of pictures of cheese plotted on the graph. assessment_items.question_content:
Laura worked as a lifeguard at the pool.  She made a graph of the number of days she worked each month.

[[☃ image 1]]

 $=5\,\text{days}$

[[☃ image 2]]

**In which month did Laura work more days than in September but fewer days than in August?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD{5}$ days.

Month | Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of days
:- | :-: | :-
May | $1$ | $\blueD{5} \times 1=5$
June | $3$ | $\blueD{5} \times 3=15$
July | $5$ | $\blueD{5} \times 5=25$
$\maroonD{\text{August}}$ | $4$ | $\blueD{5} \times 4=\maroonD{20}$
$\greenD{\text{September}}$ | $1$ | $\blueD{5} \times 1=\greenD{5}$

Laura worked as a lifeguard for $\greenD{5}$ days in September and $\maroonD{20}$ days in August.

In which month did she work between $\greenD{5}$ and $\maroonD{20}$ days?
Laura worked for $\purpleD{15}$ days in $\purpleD{\text{June}}$.
In $\purpleD{\text{June}}$, Laura worked  more days than in September but fewer days than in August.

options.alt: A picture of a lifebuoy.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Days as lifeguard and the vertical axis labeled Month. Each Month is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: May, June, July, August, and September. The number of days as lifeguard for each month is represented by the number of pictures of lifebuoys plotted on the graph. May is represented by 1 picture of a lifebuoy, June is represented by 3 pictures of lifebuoys, July is represented by 5 pictures of lifebuoys, August is represented by 4 pictures of lifebuoys, and September is represented by 1 picture of a lifebuoy.

assessment_items.question_content:
A group of friends made a graph of the number of shooting stars each person saw last month.

[[☃ image 1]]

 $=6\,\text{shooting stars}$

[[☃ image 2]]

**Who saw more shooting stars than Lucas saw, but fewer than Mike saw?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD{6}$ shooting stars.

Person | Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of shooting stars
:- | :-: | :-
$\greenD{\text{Lucas}}$ | $4$ | $\blueD{6} \times 4=\greenD{24}$
Anna | $11$ | $\blueD{6} \times 11=66$
Jackson | $5$ | $\blueD{6} \times 5=30$
$\maroonD{\text{Mike}}$ | $7$ | $\blueD{6} \times 7=\maroonD{42}$
Simone | $9$ | $\blueD{6} \times 9=54$

$\greenD{\text{Lucas}}$ saw $\greenD{24}$ shooting stars, and $\maroonD{\text{Mike}}$ saw $\maroonD{42}$ shooting stars.

Who saw between $\greenD{24}$ and $\maroonD{42}$ shooting stars?
$\purpleD{\text{Jackson}}$ saw $\purpleD{30}$ shooting stars.
$\purpleD{\text{Jackson}}$ saw more shooting stars than Lucas saw, but fewer than Mike saw.

options.alt: A picture of a star.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of stars and the vertical axis labeled Person. Each Person is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: Simone, Mike, Jackson, Anna, and Lucas. The number of shooting stars for each person is represented by the number of pictures of stars plotted on the graph. Simone is represented by 9 pictures of stars, Mike is represented by 7 pictures of stars, Jackson is represented by 5 pictures of stars, Anna is represented by 11 pictures of stars, and Lucas is represented by 4 pictures of stars.

assessment_items.question_content:
Dairy King kept a record of all the ice cream cones sold over the summer months.


[[☃ image 1]]

 $=10\,\text{cones}$


[[☃ image 2]]

**In which month did Dairy King have more ice cream cones than in July, but fewer than in the month they sold the most?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals  $\blueD{10}$ ice cream cones.

So, each

[[☃ image 2]]

 equals $\greenD5$ ice cream cones.

Month | Number of[[☃ image 1]]| Number of cones
:- | :-: | :-
September | $4\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{10} \times 4) + \greenD5=45$
August | $5$ | $\blueD{10} \times 5={50}$
$\purpleD{\text{July}}$ | $6\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{10} \times 6) + \greenD5=\purpleD{65}$
$\maroonD{\text{June}}$ | $8$ | $\blueD{10} \times 8=\maroonD{80}$
May | $7$ | $\blueD{10} \times 7=70$

Dairy King sold $\purpleD{65}$ cones in $\purpleD{\text{July}}$.  They sold the most cones $(\maroonD{80})$ in $\maroonD{\text{June}}$.

In which month did they sell between $\purpleD{65}$ and $\maroonD{80}$ cones?
Dairy King sold $\goldD{70}$ cones in $\goldD{\text{May}}$.
In $\goldD{\text{May}}$ Dairy King sold more cones than in July, but fewer than in the month they sold the most.

options.alt: A picture of an ice cream cone equals 10 cones.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of ice cream cones and the vertical axis labeled Month. Each Month is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: May, June, July, August, and September. The number of ice cream cones for each month is represented by the number of pictures of ice cream cones plotted on the graph. May is represented by 7 pictures of ice cream cones, June is represented by 8 pictures of ice cream cones, July is represented by 6 and a half pictures of ice cream cones, August is represented by 5 pictures of ice cream cones, and September is represented by 4 and a half pictures of ice cream cones.

assessment_items.question_content:
A troop of monkeys goes on an adventure.  


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which aircraft had the same number of monkeys as the spaceship and hot air balloon combined?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{4}$ monkeys.

Aircraft | Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of monkeys
:- | :-: | :-
Helicopter | $3$ | $\blueD{4}\times3=12$ monkeys
$\maroonD{\text{Spaceship}}$ | $4$ | $\blueD{4}\times4=\maroonD{16}$ monkeys
Jet | $6$ | $\blueD{4}\times6=24$ monkeys
$\greenD{\text{Hot air balloon}}$  | $2$ | $\blueD{4}\times2=\greenD8$ monkeys

First, we add to see how many monkeys were in the $\maroonD{\text{spaceship}}$ and $\greenD{\text{hot air balloon}}$ combined.

$\maroonD {16} + \greenD {8} = \blueD{24}$

Which aircraft had $\blueD{24}$ monkeys?
The $\blueD{\text{jet}}$ had the same number of monkeys as the spaceship and hot air balloon combined.

options.alt: A picture of a monkey equals 4 monkeys.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Aircraft and the vertical axis labeled Monkeys. Each aircraft is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Helicopter, Spaceship, Jet, and Hot air balloon. The number of monkeys for each aircraft is represented by the number of pictures of monkeys plotted on the graph. Helicopter is represented by 3 pictures of monkeys, Spaceship is represented by 4 pictures of monkeys, Jet is represented by 6 pictures of monkeys, and Hot air balloon is represented by 3 pictures of monkeys.

assessment_items.question_content:
The graph shows how many mice a farmer found in her field each day for $5$ days.


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which day did the farmer find the same number of mice as on Wednesday and Thursday combined?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{2}$ mice.

Day | Number of [[☃ image 1]]| Number of mice
:- | :-: | :-:
Monday | $4$ | $\blueD{2}\times4=8$ mice
Tuesday | $3$ | $\blueD{2}\times3=6$ mice
$\maroonD{\text{Wednesday}}$ | $1$ | $\blueD{2}\times1=\maroonD2$ mice
$\greenD{\text{Thursday}}$  | $2$ | $\blueD{2}\times2=\greenD4$ mice
Friday | $1$ | $\blueD{2}\times1=2$ mice

First, we add to see how many mice were found on $\maroonD{\text{Wednesday}}$ and $\greenD{\text{Thursday}}$ combined.

$\maroonD {2} + \greenD {4} = \blueD{6}$

Which day did the farmer find $\blueD{6}$ mice?

The farmer found $\blueD6$ mice on Tuesday.
So, the farmer found the same number of mice on $\blueD{\text{Tuesday}}$ as on Wednesday and Thursday combined.

options.alt: A picture of a mouse equals 2 mice.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Day and the vertical axis labeled Mice. Each day of the week is listed along the horizontal axis from left to right as follows: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, and Friday. The number of mice for each day of the week is represented by the number of pictures of mice plotted on the graph. Monday is represented by 4 pictures of mice, Tuesday is represented by 3 pictures of mice, Wednesday is represented by 1 picture of a mouse, Thursday is represented by 2 pictures of mice, and Friday is represented by 1 picture of a mouse.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lola picked different flowers from her garden.

[[☃ image 1]]

 $= 8\text{ flowers}$


[[☃ image 2]]

**Which $2$ flowers combined did Lola pick that was the same amount of flowers picked as the Orchid and Lily flowers combined?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{8}$ flowers.

So, each 

[[☃ image 2]]

 equals $\greenD{4}$ flowers.

Type of flowers | Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of Flowers
:- | :-: | :-
$\maroonD{\text{Orchid}}$ | $4$ | $\blueD{8} \times 4=\maroonD{32}$
Rose | $5\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{8} \times 5) + \greenD4 =44$
$\purpleD{\text{Lily}}$ | $7\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{8} \times 7) + \greenD 4=\purpleD{60}$
Daisy | $6$ | $\blueD{8} \times 6=48$
Sunflower | $2\dfrac12$ | $(\blueD{8} \times 2) + \greenD4=20$

First, we add to see how many $\maroonD{\text{orchid}}$ and $\purpleD{\text{lily}}$ flowers combined were picked.

$\maroonD{32} + \purpleD {60} = \blueD{92}$

Which other pair of flowers had $\blueD{92}$ flowers picked combined?
The $\blueD{\text{daisy and rose}}$ flowers combined had the same amount of flowers picked as the orchid and lily combined.

options.alt: A picture of a flower.

A picture graph shows the horizontal axis labeled Number of flowers and the vertical axis labeled Type of flower. Each type of flower is listed along the vertical axis from bottom to top as follows: Sunflower, Daisy, Lily, Rose, and Orchid. The number of flowers for each type of flower is represented by the number of pictures of flowers plotted on the graph. Sunflower is represented by 2 and a half pictures of flowers, Daisy is represented by 6 pictures of flowers, Lily is represented by 7 and a half pictures of flowers, Rose is represented by 5 and a half pictures of flowers, and Orchid is represented by 4 pictures of flowers.

assessment_items.question_content:
Perry Pig asked his friends to vote for their favorite movie.  Then, he made a picture graph to show the results.  

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**Which chart shows the correct information from the picture graph shown above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each [[☃ image 1]]

equals $\greenD{3}$ pigs.

Favorite movie | Number of [[☃ image 1]] | Number of pigs
:- | :-: | :-:
Charlotte's Web | $4$ | ${4}\times\greenD{3}=12$ pigs
Babe | $7$ | ${7}\times\greenD{3}=21$ pigs
The Muppet Movie | $5$ | ${5}\times\greenD{3}=15$ pigs

The following chart matches the picture graph:

Favorite movie | Number of pigs 
- | :-: | -
Charlotte's Web| $12$
Babe | $21$
The Muppet Movie| $15$

assessment_items.question_content:
An apple orchard grows many types of apples. Below is a table to show how many apples were picked on Saturday.

Apple Type |  Number of apples
:- | :-: | :-:
Rome | $8$
Pink Lady | $9$
Fiji | $10$
Gala | $4$
Granny Smith | $7$


[[☃ image 1]]

**Which picture graph correctly graphs the information in the table?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{2}$ apples.

Let's use the chart to create a graph and see which option matches our graph.

[[☃ image 1]]


Apple Type| Number of apples | Number of [[☃ image 2]] 
:- | :-: | :-:
Rome | $8$ | ${8}\div\blueD2=4$ pictures
Pink Lady | $9$ | ${9}\div\blueD2=$ between $4$ and $5$ pictures
Fiji | $10$ | ${10}\div\blueD2=5$ pictures
Gala | $4$ | ${4}\div\blueD2=2$ pictures
Granny Smith | $7$ | ${7}\div\blueD2=$ between $3$ and $4$ pictures

The following picture graph matches the chart:



[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A picture of an apple equals 2 apples.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gia creates picture graphs to show the number of foxes at two nature centers.  


[[☃ image 1]]

  

**In which nature center are there $4$ more Gray foxes than Kit foxes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Each 

[[☃ image 1]]

 equals $\blueD{4}$ foxes.
Let's look at each graph and find the number of pictures for each type of fox.

##Graph 1

[[☃ image 1]]

Type of Fox | Number of [[☃ image 2]]|Number of foxes
:- | :-: | :-:
Red | $2$ | ${2}\times\blueD4=8$ foxes
Arctic | $6$ | ${6}\times\blueD4=24$ foxes
Gray | $8$ | ${8}\times\blueD4=32$ foxes
Kit | $4$ | ${4}\times\blueD4=16$ foxes

$\begin{align}
\text{Gray fox} - \text{Kit fox} &= 4\\\\
32 - 16 &\neq 4
\end{align}$

##Graph 2


[[☃ image 1]]

Type of Fox | Number of [[☃ image 2]]| Number of foxes
:- | :-: | :-:
Red | $6$ | ${6}\times\blueD4=24$ foxes
Arctic | $2$ | ${2}\times\blueD4=8$ foxes
Gray | $4$ | ${4}\times\blueD4=16$ foxes
Kit | $3$ | ${3}\times\blueD4=12$ foxes

$\begin{align}
\text{Gray fox} - \text{Kit fox} &= 4\\\\
16 - 12 &= 4
\end{align}$

This graph shows there $4$ more Gray foxes than Kit foxes:


[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A picture of a fox equals 4 foxes.
